definition: Market structure is the trend framework formed by a sequence of swing highs and swing lows. An uptrend consists of higher highs (HH) and higher lows (HL); a downtrend consists of lower highs (LH) and lower lows (LL). Structure shifts (BOS/CISD) occur when price closes beyond a key swing point with displacement, often accompanied by a Fair Value Gap, signaling a potential trend change.
identification_rules: Identify consecutive swing highs (SH) and swing lows (SL): SH has lower highs on both sides; SL has higher lows on both sides.
Uptrend: each SH is higher than previous SH (HH), each SL is higher than previous SL (HL).
Downtrend: each SH is lower than previous SH (LH), each SL is lower than previous SL (LL).
Structure shift (BOS/CISD): in a downtrend, price closes above a prior SH with displacement; in an uptrend, price closes below a prior SL with displacement.
Failure to displace: price touches a swing point but closes back inside the structure, indicating a potential reversal of bias.
Liquidity sweep: price sweeps a prior swing low/high and reverses sharply, confirming a Change in State of Delivery (CSD).
Equal highs/lows: two or more swing points at the same price level; subsequent displacement direction indicates trend continuation.
Multi-timeframe alignment: confirm structure on higher timeframe (daily, 4H) before trading on lower timeframe (15M, 10M).
SMT divergence: compare related assets (e.g., ES, NQ) at the same price level; one breaking while the other does not signals a structural divergence.
Use prior day/week highs/lows or historical swing points as reference levels.
In intermediate timeframe (1H, 4H), wait for CISD (price closes through a series of same-direction candles) to confirm higher timeframe bias.
Identify POIs (Order Block, FVG, Liquidity Sweep) on the structure timeframe for lower timeframe entry.
trading_implication: Market structure establishes the trading bias. Trade in the direction of the higher timeframe structure. After a BOS/CISD, look for entries at PD Arrays (FVG, Order Block) in the discount/premium zone. Use protected swings for stop loss and target opposite liquidity pools. Structure timeframe (1H/4H) provides POIs for lower timeframe entry; align all three layers (bias, structure, entry) for high-probability trades.
common_mistakes: Using a single candle to define structure instead of a sequence of swings.
Trading before structure is fully confirmed (HH/HL or LH/LL sequence not established).
Confusing swing points with simple highs/lows; swing points require confirmation from adjacent candles.
Ignoring close price: only touching a swing point is not enough; must close inside or outside the structure.
Failing to distinguish between a breakout and a failure to displace.
Neglecting multi-timeframe alignment: trading lower timeframe without higher timeframe confirmation.
Misinterpreting a failure swing (lower high/higher low) as a structure shift; it only indicates weakening momentum.
Entering before a liquidity sweep is complete, getting stopped out.
Using SMT as a standalone signal without lower timeframe entry model.
Trading against the higher timeframe trend based on a lower timeframe BOS.
Ignoring displacement strength when evaluating structure shifts.
Confusing reversal and continuation: waiting for continuation confirmation after a potential reversal.

related_concepts.term: Change in the State of Delivery

merge_notes: School conflict: 2 ICT vs 1 Market Structure; picked ICT by majority. The Foundation project's 'Structure' card (intermediate timeframe) is merged into identification_rules and trading_implication. Definition synthesized from all three; identification_rules unioned with deduplication. Note: 'Market Structure Shift' (MSS) is treated as synonymous with BOS/CISD.
